does: This paper investigates how safety alignment in large language models transfers and generalizes across languages. The authors conduct controlled experiments using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to compare single-language versus multilingual alignment strategies. They introduce RefusEU, a new dataset of harmful prompts and safe refusals across 12 European languages, paired with a dedicated evaluation test set. Their experiments reveal that English-only alignment fails to reliably prevent harmful outputs in other languages for the same harm categories, while multilingual training simultaneously improves cross-lingual safety and maintains or improves performance on general knowledge benchmarks like Global MMLU, demonstrating that safety alignment need not come at the cost of capability degradation.
connects: This work extends research on LLM alignment and safety, which has traditionally focused on English and assumed monolingual deployment. It builds on preference optimization methods like DPO and RLHF that align model behavior with human values, but exposes a critical gap: these techniques have not been systematically evaluated for cross-lingual consistency. The paper responds to growing concerns about language-specific vulnerabilities in deployed systems and connects to broader work on multilingual NLP showing that capabilities learned in one language often do not transfer perfectly to others. It also relates to research on fairness and bias in multilingual models, extending those concerns from representation and performance disparities to safety and alignment behaviors.
enables: This work establishes multilingual refusal as a key research direction and opens several follow-up avenues. The RefusEU dataset enables systematic benchmarking of safety across European languages, supporting development of better multilingual alignment methods. The findings motivate investigation of why cross-lingual alignment fails and what mechanisms could improve transfer, such as shared safety representations or language-agnostic harm taxonomies. The approach scales to other language families and non-European languages, potentially revealing different safety transfer patterns across linguistic typologies. The work also enables research into efficient multilingual alignment—whether targeted interventions in high-transfer languages can cascade safety improvements across language families, and how to balance safety consistency with respect for cultural and linguistic diversity in global AI deployment.
